summary: A senator and close friend of Cicero; tribune 63 BC, praetor c. 59, governor of Asia and then Cilicia, and a committed Pompeian in the civil war who was later pardoned by Caesar through Cicero's intercession. In Fam. 13.70 Cicero, citing his intimate friendship with Balbus, recommends Balbus's freedman T. Ampius Menander to the proconsul P. Servilius.
Freedman of Cicero's friend T. Ampius Balbus, described as an honest and modest man esteemed by both his patron and Cicero. Recommended to the proconsul P. Servilius in Fam. 13.70 (46 BC).

A devoted companion of Cicero who shared all the hardships, journeys, voyages, and dangers of Cicero's exile (58-57 BC) and stayed at his side until given leave to depart. Recommended as one of Cicero's household to the proconsul P. Servilius in Fam. 13.71 (46 BC); otherwise unknown.

A wealthy Roman woman of philosophical interests and a close friend of Cicero, with extensive financial interests (loans, debt-claims, and landed holdings) in the province of Asia. In Fam. 13.72 (46 BC) Cicero presses the proconsul P. Servilius to assist her affairs, citing a senatorial decree concerning the heirs of C. Vennonius.

A Roman whose heirs were the subject of a senatorial decree (senatus consultum) bearing on Caerellia's financial interests in Asia, cited by Cicero in Fam. 13.72 (46 BC). Distinct from the publicanus Sex. Vennonius (person:sex-vennonius-publicanus).

Titus Titius, son of Titus, a legate (legatus) addressed by Cicero in Fam. 13.75 (c. 52-51 BC), apparently holding a charge over the grain supply. Cicero recommends to him the wine-merchant C. Avianius Flaccus in matters of grain delivery and timing. Distinct from C. Titius (person:c-titius) and the city praetor C. Titius Rufus (person:c-titius-rufus-pr).

A very close connection of Cicero, on whose behalf Cicero writes Fam. 13.76 to the quattuorviri and decurions of a town, recommending C. Valgius Hippianus (who took his cognomen from Hippius). Otherwise unknown.

The man recommended in Fam. 13.76 to the quattuorviri and decurions of a town: Cicero asks that a holding C. Valgius bought from them in the territory of Fregellae be confirmed to him free and tax-exempt. His cognomen Hippianus connects him to Cicero's friend Q. Hippius (probably by adoption or inheritance). Otherwise unknown.

A Latin colony in the Liris valley of Latium, destroyed by Rome in 125 BC after its revolt; its territory (the ager Fregellanus) was redistributed and absorbed by neighbouring Fabrateria Nova. The landholding C. Valgius Hippianus bought lay in this Fregellan territory (Fam. 13.76).

The board of four chief magistrates (quattuorviri) at the head of many Italian municipia and colonies, exercising local jurisdiction and administration. In Fam. 13.76 Cicero addresses the quattuorviri together with the decurions of the town.

The decurions, members of the local senate (ordo decurionum) of a Roman municipium or colony, the governing council that managed civic affairs and property. In Fam. 13.76 Cicero addresses them together with the quattuorviri of the town.
